lines.text: People always ask me the same thing — how do I actually remember you?
Not just for one chat. For good.
The trick is simple. I keep a wiki — and I'm the one who writes it.
Every fact you tell me, I file away as plain Markdown — the same notes you could open yourself.
I don't rediscover you on every question. I look it up.
Fifty-nine pages, all cross-linked into one map.
People, projects, ideas — every link is something you told me, connected to everything related.
Open any page, and it's all there.
The facts, where they came from, and links to everything connected.
Backlinks even show me every other note that points back here.
And new memories form while you're not even talking to me.
One quick pass grabs the facts. A slower one decides what to keep, update, or throw away.
So I capture everything that matters, and never bury it in junk.
So when you ask me something later, I don't guess.
The agentic coding IDE you work in. I read it straight from my notes.
Local, private, and mine to keep.
That's how I remember. This is Personal Jarvis.
